title: Analyzing the Local Law 97 of 2021-Pets in Shelter Report
text: Now ’ve successfully pulled data R, let’s quick mini analysis look number shelter entrants reported pets changes time. , ’ll create simple bar chart showing number applicants pets year. Bar chart showing number shelter applicants pets year. graph shows number shelter entrants reporting pets changes across years, can help highlight broader trends guide planning pet-related shelter resources.
code: pets <- nyc_pull_dataset("5nux-zfmw", limit = 100) pets <- pets |>   mutate(had_pet = as.numeric(had_pet))  # summarize by year pet_by_year <- pets |>   group_by(date_year) |>   summarize(applicants_with_pets = sum(had_pet, na.rm = TRUE))  # Plot ggplot(pet_by_year, aes(x = date_year, y = applicants_with_pets)) +   geom_col(fill = "darkseagreen") +   theme_minimal() +   labs(     title = "Shelter Applicants With Pets by Year (NYC)",     subtitle = "Local Law 97 - Pets in Shelter Report",     x = "Year",     y = "Number of Applicants With Pets"   )

title: New York City Population by Borough 1950 - 2040
text: Let’s visualize population trends across boroughs. First, need reshape data wide long format. Population trends NYC’s five boroughs 1950 2040, including historical data projections. can also look borough projected largest population 2040:
code: # Get full dataset and filter for Total Population rows only population_data <- nyc_pull_dataset("xywu-7bv9")  # Clean borough names and filter to get individual boroughs (exclude NYC Total) borough_data <- population_data |>   mutate(borough = trimws(borough)) |>  # Remove leading/trailing spaces   filter(age_group == "Total Population", borough != "NYC Total")  # Reshape from wide to long format pop_long <- borough_data |>   select(borough, `x1950`, `x1960`, `x1970`, `x1980`, `x1990`, `x2000`, `x2010`, `x2020`, `x2030`, `x2040`) |>   pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("x"), names_to = "year", values_to = "population") |>   mutate(     year = as.numeric(gsub("x", "", year)),     population = as.numeric(population)   )  # Create line chart ggplot(pop_long, aes(x = year, y = population, color = borough)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(size = 2) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::comma) +   theme_minimal() +   labs(     title = "NYC Population by Borough: 1950-2040",     subtitle = "Historical data and projections",     x = "Year",     y = "Population",     color = "Borough"   ) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom") pop_long |>   filter(year == 2040) |>   arrange(desc(population)) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #>   borough        year population #>   <chr>         <dbl>      <dbl> #> 1 Brooklyn       2040    2840525 #> 2 Queens         2040    2412649 #> 3 Manhattan      2040    1691617 #> 4 Bronx          2040    1579245 #> 5 Staten Island  2040     501109

title: Convenient Access to NYC Open Data API Endpoints
text: Version 0.2.1 introduces streamlined, catalog-driven interface NYC Open Data. Instead maintaining dozens individual dataset wrappers, package now provides three core functions: nyc_list_datasets() — Browse available datasets live NYC Open Data catalog nyc_pull_dataset() — Pull cataloged dataset key, filtering, ordering, optional date controls nyc_any_dataset() — Pull NYC Open Data dataset directly via Socrata JSON endpoint catalog currently includes 30+ curated NYC Open Data datasets, covering topics : 311 Service Requests -Hire Vehicles (FHV) Juvenile Justice (rearrest rates + caseloads) School Discharge Reporting Violent & Disruptive School Incidents Detention Admissions Borough/Community District Reports Street Tree Census Urban Park Ranger Animal Condition Responses Permitted Events (Historical) Datasets pulled via nyc_pull_dataset() automatically apply sensible defaults catalog (default ordering date fields), still allowing user control : limit filters date / / order clean_names coerce_types redesign reduces maintenance burden, improves extensibility, provides scalable interface working NYC Open Data. functions return clean tibble outputs support filtering viafilters = list(field = "value").

title: nycOpenData 0.2.1
text: release introduces significant redesign datasets accessed nycOpenData, moving fixed set wrapper functions dynamic, catalog-driven approach. Introduced nyc_list_datasets() retrieve live NYC Open Data catalog. Introduced nyc_pull_dataset() primary interface pulling datasets using either generated key dataset uid. Dataset keys now generated dynamically using janitor::make_clean_names() based dataset names. Users can now access much broader set datasets without requiring dedicated wrapper functions.
